Turbidity Post-Cal (With Lots) Exploration

Author

Perry

Published

June 11, 2026

Assumptions

  • sources of error accumulated between calibration and post-calibration are the same for all data points

    • ie. no significant differences in drift between sondes or sites

  • sources of error accumulated between calibration and post-calibration are negligible

    • ie. the expected value at post-cal remains 124 (the standard)

  • the person doing the calibrations did not significantly affect the values

  • all sondes were calibrated in the old standard and post-calibrated in either old/new standard

    • need to check this one

  • the post-cal standard was 124 for all sondes

    • mostly working with centered data (post-cal standard = 0 instead)

  • for Std_Age, Old is before lot number 24E, New is after and including

Plots

All Data

Checking for any major outliers/data quality issues.

Conclusions

There are two extreme outliers in the data. These are removed for future analyses. They are:

Program Instrument_ID Turbidity_Std Turbidity_FNU PostCalCentered PostCalDate Std_Age
WQA SM-24 23G24003826 179.43 55.43 2024-04-26 Old
WQA SM-24 24E24011758 165.68 41.68 2024-09-06 New

By Program

Get a sense of the difference between programs.

Program n
CEMP 17
WQA 85

Conclusions

WQA has substantially more data than CEMP. CEMP averaged lower values, but again, there was significantly less data. CEMP also appears to have a bimodal distribution while WQA appears unimodal.

By Std Age

Get a sense of the difference between the age of the post-cal standards.

Std_Age n_points n_stds
Old 46 6
New 56 3

Conclusions

There are roughly an equal number of data points between the old and new standards, though Old utilized more lots than New.

By Lot Number

Further break down differences between Program/Age based on the lot numbers of the standards.

Turbidity_Std n
22E24001726 1
22G22290213 1
24B24009095 2
23F24003259 3
23E24001726 4
24J24014925 9
24E24012288 13
24E24011758 34
23G24003826 35

Conclusions

All lots were exclusive to one program. Aside from 23G24003826, which had a wide spread (and the most data), anomalous values were associated with lot numbers:

  • 24E24012288 (13 points)
  • 24B24011758 (2 points)

24E24012288 has strong evidence of being a “bad” lot; there’s a decent amount of data centered on a value much lower than zero. 24B24011758 only has two points, so it’s harder to make a conclusion, but both those values are far from the expected mean. For these purposes we’ll also attribute this to a bad lot issue.

22G22290213 also had a high “mean”, but with only one slightly high data point, it’s impossible to say if the lot is truly problematic.

Note that the two extreme outlier points came from 23G24003826 (which has a wide spread) and 24E24011758 (otherwise un-problematic).

Therefore, we concluded lot number at least partially explained anomalous data. To ensure there wasn’t further issues associated with Old vs New standard, we continued with a filtered data set.

Filtered “Good” Data

Here, we filtered the data to remove lots 24B24009095 (strong evidence of issues) and 24E24012288 (weaker evidence, but still some evidence of issues).

Std_Age n_points n_stds
New 43 2
Old 44 5

Conclusions

With the problematic lots filtered out, the difference between Old and New standard (and their differences from the true standard value, here centered at 0) appear significantly lessened. The number of data points within each group are also now almost exactly equal, though Old still includes more lots than New. Old also has a few more outliers than New.

Model

Next we do a simple linear model to check for differences in mean post-cal values:

  • between Std Ages (is the Old mean different from New?)
  • from the true standard value (are Old/New means different from 124?)

Model Results


Call:
lm(formula = PostCalCentered ~ Std_Age, data = df_good)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-10.1214  -0.9261   0.1986   1.4386   8.6986 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)  
(Intercept)  -0.7791     0.4207  -1.852   0.0675 .
Std_AgeOld    1.1504     0.5916   1.945   0.0551 .
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 2.759 on 85 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.04259,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.03133 
F-statistic: 3.781 on 1 and 85 DF,  p-value: 0.05514

EMM Results

contrast estimate SE df t.ratio p.value
New - Old -1.150433 0.5916325 85 -1.944507 0.0551414
Std_Age emmean SE df lower.CL upper.CL
New -0.7790698 0.4207448 85 -1.6156231 0.0574836
Old 0.3713636 0.4159361 85 -0.4556288 1.1983561

Testing Mean = 0

Std_Age emmean SE df t.ratio p.value
New -0.7790698 0.4207448 85 -1.8516445 0.0675491
Old 0.3713636 0.4159361 85 0.8928382 0.3744647

Conclusions

We modeled the Post Cal differences by the Standard Age with the problematic lot batches removed. The main model gave weak evidence against a significant difference between the two Age groups. Furthermore, neither group has a significant difference from 0; this conclusion is stronger for Old and weaker for New.

However, even for New, the mean difference is only 0.8 units below 0 (ie. a mean of ~123 instead of 124). Even if this was considered real, it’s not a large enough difference to worry about.

Data Variance

We conclude that, once “bad” lots are removed, all post cal data (both with Old and New standard) are centered around the standard value of 124. However, if the variance around this mean is large, a high percentage of data could still be flagged anomalous. We graph this to check:

Conclusions

Both Old and New standard data look similar. For Old, 50% of the centered data lies between (-0.59, 1.73). For New, it’s between (-1.62, 0.88). Conservatively, this implies half the data lies between ~(122, 126). Furthermore, for both, ~90% of the centered data lies, conservatively, between (-5,5), meaning that post cal values below/above (119, 129) are rare.

Overall Conclusions

Lot Numbers explain the majority of issues with post-calibration results. Two lots, 24E24012288 and 24B24011758, produced post-cal values that were far from the true standard value, indicating those batches had quality issues. After accounting for these lots, there was no significant difference between Old and New standard and neither significantly differed from the true value.

We also looked at the variance around the remaining “good” data. Conservatively, 50% was within 2 units of the true standard value (122, 126) and 90% of the data was still within 5 (119, 129).
